lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — What cancer actually is
Alright, let's get started.
Good morning, everyone.

For the next two hours we're going to talk about cancer — and I want to begin not with statistics, not with a famous patient, not with a chart of mortality rates, but with a single picture in your head that I want you to carry through the whole lecture.
Think of your body as a city.
A really big city.
Tens of trillions of citizens, each one a cell.
And the astonishing thing about this city — the thing that's so easy to take for granted that you've probably never noticed it — is that every single one of those citizens cooperates.
Your liver cells stay in your liver.
Your skin cells divide just often enough to replace what gets shed and then stop.
Your gut lining cells, which turn over completely every few days, follow precise rules about when to divide, where to go, and when to politely die to make room for their replacements.
Every cell in your body, every day, is doing a job for the larger whole.
The body is a cooperative.
Cancer is what happens when one cell defects from the cooperative.
It stops following the rules.
It divides when it shouldn't, it goes where it shouldn't, it doesn't die when it's supposed to.
And — this is the crucial part — when that one renegade cell divides, its children inherit the same defection.
They divide too.
They become a lineage.
A growing colony of citizens who have decided the rules don't apply to them.
So here's the single most useful frame for everything we'll do today: cancer is a cell that has forgotten the rules of cooperation, and then made copies of itself.
That's it.
That's the disease.
Everything else — the mutations, the drugs, the screening tests, the immunotherapies — is built on top of that one idea.
Cancer is, fundamentally, an evolutionary problem playing out inside your body.
A new cellular species, descended from one of your own cells, that has acquired the ability to grow at your expense.
Now, that framing immediately tells you something important.
Cancer is not an invader.
Unlike a virus or a bacterium, cancer doesn't come from outside.
It comes from you.
The cells of a breast tumor are descendants of cells that were, at some recent point, perfectly well-behaved breast cells.
They share your DNA, your metabolism, your surface markers.
That's why cancer is so hard to treat — your immune system has spent a lifetime learning not to attack your own cells, and cancer is, biologically, your own cells.
It is a problem of distinguishing self from slightly-corrupted self.
And cancer is common.
Roughly one in two of us, over a lifetime in a wealthy country, will be diagnosed with some form of it.

Not because the world is uniquely hostile to us, but for a sobering reason we'll come back to: cancer is largely a disease of accumulated mistakes, and the longer you live, the more mistakes accumulate.
We have a cancer epidemic partly because we no longer die of the things that used to kill us first.

If you walk out of here in two hours and you can do three things — one, explain to a friend without jargon what cancer is at the cellular level; two, sketch the broad categories of how cancer cells differ from normal ones and how drugs exploit each difference; three, look at a news headline about a "new cancer breakthrough" and have an honest sense of what it really means — then we've succeeded.
Everything else is detail hung onto that scaffold.
Let's build the scaffold.
